title: Baseline retest for cores/bench
Guard ALU multipliers off critical path for non-M ops
motivation:
The baseline ALU unconditionally computes three 64-bit products on every
cycle, even for simple ADD/SUB/AND/OR/XOR/SLT/SLTU/SLL/SRL/SRA/LUI
operations. On the Tang Nano 20K (GW2AR-18C) these inferred multipliers
sit on the combinational critical path and limit Fmax. CoreMark is
dominated by integer ALU and memory ops, so the multiplier is almost
never exercised, yet it still degrades timing for every other operation.
Removing it from the critical path should raise Fmax with zero IPC risk.

Removing it from the critical path should raise Fmax with zero IPC risk.
hypothesis.hypothesis:
Restructure alu.sv so the 64-bit multiply assignments (mul_ss, mul_uu,
mul_su) are only evaluated when the ALU operation is MUL, MULH, MULHU,
or MULHSU. For all other opcodes the products are tied to zero inside
the always_comb block. This tells the synthesis tool that the multiplier
hardware is not needed on the fast-path case items (ADD, SUB, logic,
shifts, etc.), shortening the critical path and raising Fmax.

changes.description: Guard the 64-bit multiply computations inside a conditional case block so they only execute for MUL/MULH/MULHU/MULHSU ops. Default the product variables to zero so the main output case statement remains unchanged.

lesson: - 2026-05-14 hyp-20260514-001-r1s0 (improvement, +11.46%): On inference FPGA targets, unconditionally computing wide multiply products inside the ALU keeps multiplier hardware on the combinational critical path for non-M opcodes; gating the multiply evaluation by opcode removes it from the fast path and raises Fmax with no IPC penalty.

title: Cold RV32M High-Half and Divide Unit
motivation:
The baseline ALU unconditionally builds three independent 64-bit multiply
products (signed*signed, unsigned*unsigned, signed*unsigned) and a
combinational 32-bit divider on every cycle. This bloats area to ~9500 LUT4
and places heavy arithmetic on the EX-stage critical path for *all*
instructions, even simple ADD or BRANCH. CoreMark's timed path is multiply-
heavy but only uses the low-half MUL result; it is effectively cold for
DIV/REM and high-half multiplies (MULH/MULHU/MULHSU). Prior work on similar
RV32IM baselines showed that moving cold division off the universal ALU path
was one of the largest pure Fmax wins. Extending that idea to also remove the
two unused high-half multipliers should compound the area and timing benefit
without touching the benchmark's hot IPC path.

hypothesis.motivation:
The baseline ALU unconditionally builds three independent 64-bit multiply
products (signed*signed, unsigned*unsigned, signed*unsigned) and a
combinational 32-bit divider on every cycle. This bloats area to ~9500 LUT4
and places heavy arithmetic on the EX-stage critical path for *all*
instructions, even simple ADD or BRANCH. CoreMark's timed path is multiply-
heavy but only uses the low-half MUL result; it is effectively cold for
DIV/REM and high-half multiplies (MULH/MULHU/MULHSU). Prior work on similar
RV32IM baselines showed that moving cold division off the universal ALU path
was one of the largest pure Fmax wins. Extending that idea to also remove the
two unused high-half multipliers should compound the area and timing benefit
without touching the benchmark's hot IPC path.
hypothesis.hypothesis:
Restructure the EX stage into a fast single-cycle integer ALU plus a
dedicated cold Multiply-Divide Unit (MDU).

- The ALU keeps ADD/SUB/logic/shift/compare/LUI and the existing single-
  cycle low-half MUL (mul_uu[31:0]). All other M-extension opcodes are
  removed from the ALU result mux.
- The new `rtl/mdu.sv` module handles the cold ops:
    * DIV/DIVU/REM/REMU using a 32-cycle iterative restoring divider.
    * MULH/MULHU/MULHSU using a 2-cycle pipelined 33x33 multiplier whose
      high-half result is captured in a registered output.
  The divider reuses the proven `div_unit.sv` state machine pattern from
  the reference v1 core, adapted for the bench baseline.
- `rtl/ex_stage.sv` detects a cold M-extension opcode and asserts a new
  `ex_long_busy` signal to the hazard unit, freezing the pipeline until the
  MDU signals `done`. On completion the MDU result is written into the
  EX/MEM register exactly like a normal ALU result.
- The ALTOPS branch in the MDU mirrors the formal substitutions already
  present in the baseline ALU, so `make formal` behavior is unchanged.

CoreMark should see zero IPC penalty because its timed loop contains no
DIV/REM and no MULH variants, while the common RV32I and MUL instructions
now run on a shorter critical path. LUT utilization should drop by
~1500-2500, relieving routing congestion and raising median Fmax.

changes.description:
Remove DIV/DIVU/REM/REMU cases and MULH/MULHU/MULHSU cases from the
main result mux. Keep only the hot RV32I ops plus ALU_MUL (low half
of mul_uu). Add a cold-op indicator bit or reuse op[4] so EX stage
can detect when the MDU is needed.

New module: registered 2-cycle high-half multiplier for MULH/MULHU/
MULHSU plus a 32-cycle iterative divider for DIV/DIVU/REM/REMU.
Includes start/busy/done handshake, ALTOPS substitutions under
`RISCV_FORMAL_ALTOPS`, and exact RV32M corner-case handling.

Instantiate MDU, route operands to it, detect cold M-op via opcode
encoding, assert `ex_long_busy` while MDU is active, and select MDU
result into EX/MEM when done.

Accept `ex_long_busy` alongside existing `dmem_ready` / `imem_ready`
stall sources. Treat MDU busy like a dmem stall: freeze IF/ID/EX
and bubble EX/MEM until the unit completes.

Wire the new `ex_long_busy` signal from ex_stage into hazard_unit.
No top-level IO changes.

title: 32-entry 2-bit dynamic branch predictor in IF stage
motivation:
The baseline always fetches pc+4 and only redirects after a branch resolves in EX,
costing a 2-cycle bubble for every taken branch. CoreMark is loop-heavy; even a
simple dynamic predictor eliminates most of these bubbles, directly improving IPC.
A 32-entry PHT adds only 64 flops and a small combinational read port, well within
the ~400 LUT4 headroom on the Tang Nano 20K.

hypothesis.motivation:
The baseline always fetches pc+4 and only redirects after a branch resolves in EX,
costing a 2-cycle bubble for every taken branch. CoreMark is loop-heavy; even a
simple dynamic predictor eliminates most of these bubbles, directly improving IPC.
A 32-entry PHT adds only 64 flops and a small combinational read port, well within
the ~400 LUT4 headroom on the Tang Nano 20K.
hypothesis.hypothesis:
Add a 32-entry 2-bit saturating counter branch predictor (PHT) indexed by PC[6:2].
In the IF stage, detect conditional branches (opcode 1100011) and JAL (opcode 1101111);
extract the immediate via a small imm_gen instance and compute target = pc + imm.
Query the PHT for branches; always predict JAL as taken. A new predict_taken bit is
added to the IF/ID and ID/EX pipeline bundles. In EX, redirect is asserted only on
mispredict (predicted != actual outcome), with the redirect target selecting the
correct path (branch target or pc+4). The PHT is updated when a branch resolves in EX.

title: Pre-compute branch target and pc+4 in earlier stages
motivation:
The EX stage currently computes two 32-bit additions on its critical path:
branch_target = pc + imm (for branches and JAL) and pc + 4 (for JAL/JALR
return address and pc_next on not-taken paths). These adders sit between
the ID/EX register and the redirect/pc_next muxes, lengthening the path
from branch resolution back to the IF stage PC register. By pre-computing
these values in IF (pc+4) and ID (pc+imm) and carrying them through the
pipeline registers, we remove two 32-bit adders from the EX combinational
cloud. This shortens the redirect critical path and should raise worst-case
Fmax seeds without any IPC change.

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Add pc_plus4 to if_id_t and id_ex_t, and add branch_target to id_ex_t.
In if_stage, compute out.pc_plus4 = pc + 4. In id_stage, compute
branch_target = in.pc + imm and capture both pc_plus4 and branch_target
into the ID/EX register. In ex_stage, replace every occurrence of
in.pc + 4 with in.pc_plus4 and every occurrence of in.pc + in.imm with
in.branch_target. This removes the pc+4 and pc+imm adders from EX,
leaving only the JALR rs1+imm adder and the ALU itself.

lesson: - 2026-05-14 hyp-20260514-001-r2s0 (regression, -8.01%): Pre-computing pc+4 in IF and pc+imm in ID to remove adders from EX backfires because the adders were previously hidden in parallel with the ALU's longer delay; moving them upstream creates new critical paths in lighter stages and worsens overall Fmax.

title: Early branch and jump resolution in ID stage
motivation:
The current 5-stage pipeline resolves all control-flow instructions in EX,
causing a 2-cycle bubble for every taken branch and jump. CoreMark's
tight loops and frequent function calls spend a significant fraction of
cycles recovering from these mispredicts. Moving branch condition
evaluation and jump target computation to the ID stage cuts the penalty
to 1 cycle, directly improving iterations/cycle.

evaluation and jump target computation to the ID stage cuts the penalty
to 1 cycle, directly improving iterations/cycle.
hypothesis.hypothesis:
Relocate unconditional jump (JAL/JALR) target calculation and conditional
branch resolution from EX to ID. Add operand forwarding paths from
EX/MEM and MEM/WB into the ID stage so branches dependent on recent
results resolve without stalling. Introduce a load-to-branch stall in
the hazard unit when a branch in ID consumes a load result still in EX.
Pipeline an early-redirect flag so EX does not re-assert flush on an
instruction already redirected in ID.

title: Static backward-taken predictor with early JAL resolution in IF
motivation:
The baseline fetches sequentially and resolves all control flow in EX,
incurring a 2-cycle bubble for every taken branch and unconditional jump.
CoreMark is loop-heavy, so backward conditional branches (loop edges)
dominate the dynamic branch count. A purely static predictor that predicts
JAL as always-taken and backward branches as taken requires no stateful
table, no PHT updates, and minimal combinational logic in the IF stage.
Because the prediction is computed from the instruction already on the
imem_data bus, the target can be fetched immediately—eliminating the
redirect bubble entirely for correct predictions.

hypothesis.motivation:
The baseline fetches sequentially and resolves all control flow in EX,
incurring a 2-cycle bubble for every taken branch and unconditional jump.
CoreMark is loop-heavy, so backward conditional branches (loop edges)
dominate the dynamic branch count. A purely static predictor that predicts
JAL as always-taken and backward branches as taken requires no stateful
table, no PHT updates, and minimal combinational logic in the IF stage.
Because the prediction is computed from the instruction already on the
imem_data bus, the target can be fetched immediately—eliminating the
redirect bubble entirely for correct predictions.
hypothesis.hypothesis:
Add combinational branch/jump prediction to the IF stage and suppress
EX-stage redirects when the prediction is correct.

1. Extend if_id_t and id_ex_t with a single predict_taken bit.
2. In if_stage, instantiate a small imm_gen (or inline equivalent) to
   extract the B/J immediate from imem_data. Detect opcode 1100011
   (BRANCH) and 1101111 (JAL). Predict taken for JAL unconditionally,
   and for branches when the immediate sign bit is 1 (backward branch).
   On a predicted-taken instruction, redirect next_pc to pc+imm instead
   of pc+4. This means the sequential fall-through instruction is never
   fetched, so no flush is needed on a correct prediction.
3. In id_stage, pass predict_taken through the ID/EX register.
4. In ex_stage, change the redirect condition so it only fires on a
   mispredict: for branches when branch_taken != predict_taken, and for
   jumps when predict_taken == 0 (i.e. JALR, which is never predicted).
   Correctly predicted JALs and backward branches therefore flow through
   the pipeline with zero bubble penalty.

This is a much simpler and lower-risk version of the broken 32-entry
dynamic-PHT attempt: no stateful structures, no PHT update logic, and a
tiny combinational path (opcode compare → imm mux → adder) that reuses
the existing pc+4 adder delay budget.

title: MEM-stage load forwarding to eliminate load-use stalls
motivation:
The baseline 5-stage pipeline stalls for one cycle on every load-use hazard
because the load data is only available after it is latched into MEM/WB.
CoreMark is pointer- and array-heavy, so these stalls are frequent.
By forwarding the combinational load data directly from the MEM stage to
the EX-stage operand muxes, the dependent instruction can enter EX on the
very next cycle without any bubble, directly improving IPC.

hypothesis.motivation:
The baseline 5-stage pipeline stalls for one cycle on every load-use hazard
because the load data is only available after it is latched into MEM/WB.
CoreMark is pointer- and array-heavy, so these stalls are frequent.
By forwarding the combinational load data directly from the MEM stage to
the EX-stage operand muxes, the dependent instruction can enter EX on the
very next cycle without any bubble, directly improving IPC.
hypothesis.hypothesis:
Add a combinational `load_data_out` port to `mem_stage` that exposes the
internally computed, shifted/sign-extended load result. In `core.sv`, change
the `fwd_ex_mem` connection fed into `ex_stage` from a plain
`ex_mem_w.alu_result` to the multiplexed value
`ex_mem_w.ctrl.mem_read ? mem_load_data : ex_mem_w.alu_result`.
Because the existing `forward_unit` already asserts select `2'd1` whenever
the EX/MEM instruction writes the matching `rd`, the EX-stage operand mux
will automatically pick up the actual load data instead of the memory
address.  Finally, remove the `load_use_hazard` logic from `hazard_unit`
(and its now-unused inputs `id_ex_mem_read`, `id_ex_rd`, `if_id_rs1`,
`if_id_rs2`) so the pipeline no longer inserts a bubble for this case.

changes.description:
Add an output port `logic [31:0] load_data_out` and assign it the
internal `load_data` value (the shifted/sign-extended load result
computed combinationally from `dmem_rdata`). No other logic changes.

1. Create a new top-level wire `logic [31:0] mem_load_data` and connect
   it to `u_mem.load_data_out`.
2. Change the `.fwd_ex_mem` port connection into `u_ex` from
   `ex_mem_w.alu_result` to
   `ex_mem_w.ctrl.mem_read ? mem_load_data : ex_mem_w.alu_result`.

title: Eliminate load-use stall with MEM-to-EX forwarding
motivation:
The current 5-stage pipeline stalls ID by one cycle on every load-use
hazard (a LOAD in EX followed by an instruction in ID that reads the
LOAD's rd).  CoreMark's pointer-chasing, list-walk, and matrix-access
patterns contain a high density of back-to-back load-dependent ALU ops,
so this bubble is a significant IPC limiter.  Branch-related structural
changes have already been exhausted with neutral or negative results;
the data-forwarding network is the remaining untouched structural knob.
By forwarding the sign/zero-extended load data directly from the
combinational MEM-stage output to the EX-stage operand muxes, the
dependent instruction can enter EX in the cycle immediately after the
LOAD moves to MEM, eliminating the stall entirely.

title: 32-entry direct-mapped BTB in IF stage to eliminate taken-branch bubbles
motivation:
The baseline always fetches sequentially, paying a 2-cycle bubble for every
taken branch and JAL. A prior static predictor that computed branch targets
combinationally in IF from imem_data (decode -> imm_gen -> adder) slashed
Fmax by ~40% because that path starts from the off-chip/BRAM instruction
word. A Branch Target Buffer (BTB) avoids that path entirely: it is indexed
directly by the PC register output, so the predicted next PC is just a small
distributed-RAM read with no instruction decode or adder in the critical
path. On the Tang Nano 20K a 32-entry BTB costs only ~35 LUTs and reads in
a single LUT delay, making it a much safer timing bet while still removing
the branch bubble for loop-back edges once they warm up.

- Read: combinational, indexed by pc[6:2], outputs hit and target[31:0].
- Write: registered in EX when a conditional branch or JAL is resolved
  taken, storing pc[6:2] -> target.
- IF stage: next_pc = redirect ? redirect_target : btb_hit ? btb_target : pc+4.

the previous static predictor's imem_data->decode->adder path, so Fmax

title: Forward exact load result from MEM to EX and remove load-use stall
motivation:

because the load data is only available after it is latched into MEM/WB.

the load_use_hazard stall logic, the dependent instruction can enter EX on the
very next cycle with the exact same value that will later be written to the
regfile.

title: 64-entry tagged BTB inlined in IF with mispredict-only redirect
motivation:
Dynamic profiling shows CoreMark executes ~360k taken control-flow
instructions (branches + JAL + JALR) out of 2.9M total instructions.
The baseline always predicts not-taken, paying a 2-cycle bubble for
every one of them.  Eliminating these bubbles would cut ~718k cycles
from the 4.5M-cycle run, yielding a ~16% IPC improvement.

A prior 32-entry direct-mapped BTB attempt produced exactly zero
fitness delta with identical LUT/Fmax numbers, strongly suggesting the
BTB was synthesized away because the update path or the next-pc mux
was not wired correctly.  This hypothesis inlines the BTB directly
into if_stage.sv (no separate module to forget to instantiate), adds
a partial tag to avoid aliasing, and explicitly spells out the exact
next-pc expression and the pc+4 mispredict-recovery path so the
implementation agent cannot leave it as a no-op.

target.  The read is combinational: index = pc[7:2], hit = valid &&
(tag == pc[31:8]).  The write is synchronous and driven from the
EX/MEM register via top-level wiring in core.sv.

2. In if_stage.sv, declare three parallel arrays btb_valid[0:63],
   btb_tag[0:63][23:0], btb_target[0:63][31:0].  On reset clear all
   valid bits.  Combinational read produces btb_hit and btb_target.
   Override next_pc as:
     next_pc = redirect ? redirect_target
                        : (btb_hit ? btb_target : pc + 4);
   Set out.predict_taken = btb_hit.
   Add input ports btb_update_en, btb_update_pc[31:0],
   btb_update_target[31:0] and register the write on the posedge.
3. In id_stage.sv, latch in.predict_taken into the ID/EX register.
4. In ex_stage.sv, replace the unconditional redirect with
   mispredict-only redirect:
     actual_taken = branch_taken || in.ctrl.is_jump;
     redirect     = (actual_taken != in.predict_taken) && !misalign_fault;

title: Gate divider evaluation by opcode in ALU
motivation:
The ALU currently gates 64-bit multiply product evaluation behind an
opcode check, which was proven in a prior iteration to raise Fmax by
removing the multiplier from the critical path of non-M instructions.
The divide/remainder operations (DIV/DIVU/REM/REMU) are still computed
inside the case statement without an explicit input gate. On FPGA
inference targets the divider is a large combinational cell whose
physical path from a/b to out is analyzed by the STA tool even when
the current instruction is not a divide, keeping the divider on the
critical path for ADD/SHIFT/LOGIC operations. CoreMark rarely (if ever)
uses division, so any IPC impact is negligible.

Introduce intermediate variables (div_result, divu_result, rem_result,
remu_result) and guard their computation with the same if-gate pattern
already used for multiplies: only evaluate the `/` and `%` operators
when op is ALU_DIV/ALU_DIVU/ALU_REM/ALU_REMU.  The case statement then
selects the pre-computed variables.  This physically disconnects the
divider inputs for non-DIV instructions, shortening the worst-case
combinational path and raising Fmax with no architectural change.

implementation_notes:
# Implementation Notes — Gate Divider Evaluation by Opcode in ALU

## What changed

Edited `cores/bench/rtl/alu.sv` only:

1. Added four intermediate `logic [31:0]` variables: `div_result`, `divu_result`, `rem_result`, `remu_result`.
2. Placed their computation inside an opcode-guarded `if` block that only evaluates when `op` is `ALU_DIV`, `ALU_DIVU`, `ALU_REM`, or `ALU_REMU`.  The block defaults all four results to `32'b0` when the guard is false, mirroring the existing multiply-gating pattern (`mul_ss`/`mul_uu`/`mul_su`).
3. Replaced the inline `/` and `%` operators in the non-ALTOPS `case` items with references to the pre-computed variables:
   - `ALU_DIV`  → `div_result`
   - `ALU_DIVU` → `divu_result`
   - `ALU_REM`  → `rem_result`
   - `ALU_REMU` → `remu_result`
4. Left the `RISCV_FORMAL_ALTOPS` branch untouched (it uses XOR-of-add/sub stand-ins and has no real divider logic).

## Deviations

None. The change follows the proposed hypothesis exactly.

## Concerns

None. The semantics of every divide/remainder edge case (divide-by-zero, `INT_MIN/-1` overflow) are preserved bit-for-bit with the original inline expressions. The only effect is physical: the divider inputs are disconnected from the combinational path when the ALU opcode is not a divide/remainder, which should shorten the critical path for ADD/SHIFT/LOGIC operations and raise Fmax on inference targets.

## Verification

- **Verilator lint**: `verilator --lint-only -Wall -Wno-MULTITOP -sv +incdir+cores/bench/rtl cores/bench/rtl/*.sv` — clean, no warnings/errors.
- **Local formal**: `RTL_DIR=cores/bench/rtl CORE_NAME=bench bash formal/run_all.sh` — **53 passed, 0 failed** (auto-detected `nret=1`, used `wrapper_si.sv` + `checks_si.cfg`).
lesson: - 2026-05-14 hyp-20260514-001-r5s0 (regression, -5.99%): Explicit opcode gating of divide/remainder operations regressed Fmax because FPGA synthesis tools already optimize case-statement divider inference, and the added opcode-comparison and pre-compute mux logic added combinational delay that outweighed any path reduction; timing micro-optimizations that work for multiplies do not automatically transfer to dividers.

title: 8-entry direct-mapped instruction cache in IF to absorb imem stalls
motivation:
The fitness metric multiplies FPGA Fmax (measured with zero-wait imem) by
CoreMark IPC (measured under ~22% random imem stalls). The baseline stalls
the entire front-end on every imem backpressure cycle, inserting a bubble.
An 8-entry direct-mapped instruction cache in the IF stage can serve cached
instructions during stalls, improving IPC. Because the FPGA bench ties
io_imemReady to 1'b1, the cache-read path is dead in synthesis and will be
pruned by Yosys, leaving Fmax and area unchanged.

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Add an 8-entry direct-mapped instruction cache inside if_stage. The cache
is indexed by pc[4:2] with a 27-bit tag (pc[31:5]). On each cycle where
imem_ready is high and there is no redirect, the fetched instruction updates
the indexed entry. A new combinational icache_hit output is driven when
the current PC matches a valid tag. hazard_unit receives icache_hit and
suppresses imem_stall when the cache hits: imem_stall = !imem_ready &&
!icache_hit. Consequently stall_if and flush_if are not asserted on a
cache hit, allowing the PC to advance and the pipeline to consume the
cached instruction even while the bus is stalled. The cache data path is
explicitly gated by !imem_ready so formal and FPGA zero-wait modes always
use the live imem_data and never see stale cached values.

imem_ready. Update cache on imem_ready && !redirect. Modify the
combinational output so that when !imem_ready && icache_hit the
stage emits the cached instruction and keeps valid high; otherwise
retain baseline behavior (NOP on flush, imem_data when ready).

Add input icache_hit. Change imem_stall from !imem_ready to

Wire io_imemReady into if_stage as imem_ready. Wire the new

title: 16-entry inlined BTB with explicit RVFI-safe mispredict-only redirect
motivation:
The baseline pays a 2-cycle bubble for every taken branch and JAL because
it always fetches PC+4 and redirects only after EX resolution. Prior
attempts to add a BTB either produced zero fitness delta (identical LUT/FF
counts prove the structure was synthesized away because the update path was
disconnected) or broke rvfi_pc_fwd (the implementation agent altered
EX/MEM.pc_next to depend on redirect, so a correct prediction reported
PC+4 instead of the actual target). A very small, explicitly inlined BTB
avoids module-instantiation mistakes, and spelling out the exact combinational
changes in ex_stage.sv with a bold “do not touch pc_next” rule should finally
let the predictor actually fire without corrupting the architectural trace.
CoreMark is loop-heavy; even a 16-entry table should capture the handful of
hot backward edges and JAL targets that dominate dynamic control flow.

title: Forward exact load_data from MEM to EX to eliminate load-use stalls
motivation:
The 5-stage pipeline currently stalls for one cycle on every load-use hazard.
CoreMark contains frequent pointer-chasing and array indexing patterns that
trigger these stalls. Previous attempts failed because the forwarded value
did not exactly match the WB write value. mem_stage already computes the
fully byte-aligned and sign-extended load_data combinationally; exposing it
to the EX-stage forward mux allows us to remove the stall entirely without
duplicating extraction logic.

title: Static BTFN predictor in IF with mispredict-only redirect in EX
motivation:
The baseline always predicts not-taken, paying a 2-cycle flush penalty on every
taken branch and every JAL. CoreMark is loop-heavy and makes frequent function
calls, so backward branches and JALs are a large fraction of dynamic control-flow.
A stateless Backward-Taken / Forward-Not-taken (BTFN) predictor costs almost no
state, adds only a small amount of combinational logic in the IF stage, and can
eliminate the flush penalty on loop back-edges and unconditional jumps.

hypothesis.motivation:
The baseline always predicts not-taken, paying a 2-cycle flush penalty on every
taken branch and every JAL. CoreMark is loop-heavy and makes frequent function
calls, so backward branches and JALs are a large fraction of dynamic control-flow.
A stateless Backward-Taken / Forward-Not-taken (BTFN) predictor costs almost no
state, adds only a small amount of combinational logic in the IF stage, and can
eliminate the flush penalty on loop back-edges and unconditional jumps.
hypothesis.hypothesis:
Add a BTFN predictor to the IF stage:
  - JAL is always predicted taken (target = PC + JAL imm, known in IF).
  - Conditional branches are predicted taken only when their immediate is negative
    (backward branch), otherwise not-taken.
  - JALR is not predicted (falls through) because the target depends on a register
    value not visible in IF.
  - The predicted target is validated in the same cycle it is produced; if the
    computed target is misaligned ([1:0] != 0) the prediction is suppressed and
    IF falls back to PC+4, matching the trap-and-fallthrough semantics.

Change the EX-stage redirect semantics from "taken branch/jump" to "mispredict":
  - EX always computes the architecturally correct next-PC (pc_next / pc_wdata)
    from the actual branch/jump resolution, independent of prediction.
  - redirect is asserted only when the actual outcome differs from the BTFN
    prediction (JALR always mispredicts, backward branches mispredict when not
    taken, forward branches mispredict when taken).
  - redirect_target is driven from the actual resolved target, not the prediction.
  - This preserves RVFI correctness because pc_wdata never depends on the
    predictor state.

With this change, correctly predicted JALs and loop-back branches cost 0 flush
cycles instead of 2, directly improving CoreMark IPC.

changes.description: Insert BTFN prediction combinational logic before the PC register. Compute JAL target and BRANCH target from the raw fetched instruction. Drive next_pc from the predicted target for JAL and backward branches, unless the target is misaligned. Pass no extra state through the pipeline bundle; EX recomputes the prediction locally.

Replace redirect = (branch_taken || is_jump) with mispredict detection: JAL is never a mispredict, JALR is always a mispredict, conditional branches mispredict when branch_taken != imm[31]. Drive redirect_target and pc_next from the actual resolved target (same as architectural next-PC). Ensure misalign_fault still suppresses redirect and forces pc_next = PC+4.

Insert BTFN prediction combinational logic before the PC register. Compute JAL target and BRANCH target from the raw fetched instruction. Drive next_pc from the predicted target for JAL and backward branches, unless the target is misaligned. Pass no extra state through the pipeline bundle; EX recomputes the prediction locally.

Replace redirect = (branch_taken || is_jump) with mispredict detection: JAL is never a mispredict, JALR is always a mispredict, conditional branches mispredict when branch_taken != imm[31]. Drive redirect_target and pc_next from the actual resolved target (same as architectural next-PC). Ensure misalign_fault still suppresses redirect and forces pc_next = PC+4.

title: Forward exact load_data from MEM to EX to eliminate load-use stalls
motivation:
Load-use stalls are the primary IPC bottleneck in the 5-stage baseline.
A LOAD followed by a dependent ALU instruction currently stalls for one
cycle.  Previous MEM-to-EX forwarding attempts failed because they
forwarded raw dmem_rdata (wrong for byte/halfword loads) or added
unused ports to forward_unit (compile failure).  By replicating the
full mem_stage byte-alignment and sign-extension logic in the
top-level forwarding path, the forwarded value matches the WB write
value exactly, satisfying RVFI.  Re-using the existing forward_unit
encoding (EX_MEM=2'd1) avoids port changes.

hypothesis.hypothesis:
In core.sv, replicate mem_stage's load_data computation
combinationally and drive it onto fwd_ex_mem whenever EX/MEM holds a
load (mem_read=1).  This lets the existing forward_unit and ex_stage
muxes route the loaded value to a dependent instruction in EX,
eliminating the one-cycle load-use stall for all load widths.  In
hazard_unit.sv, clear load_use_hazard so the pipeline no longer
stalls on any load-use.  During a dmem stall the pipeline still
freezes via dmem_stall, so the forwarding path is only active when

title: Merge ID and EX into single 4-stage pipeline
motivation:
The current 5-stage pipeline pays a 2-cycle penalty for every taken branch and
unconditional jump because branch resolution happens in EX, flushing IF and ID.
CoreMark has a significant fraction of control-flow instructions; collapsing ID
and EX into one combinational stage cuts the mispredict/jump penalty to 1 cycle.
In addition, by exposing the MEM stage's extracted load_data as a forward source,
load-use hazards can be resolved via forwarding rather than stall, eliminating the
1-cycle load-use bubble entirely.

generation, operand forwarding, ALU execution, and branch resolution all in one
combinational cloud, capturing results in a single pipeline register that feeds
MEM.  The mem_stage gains a combinational forward_data output (mem_to_reg ?
load_data : alu_result) so the forward_unit can source the freshest register
value from the MEM stage itself, removing the need for load-use stalls.
hazard_unit is simplified: load_use detection is removed, dmem stalls freeze the
ID/EX register and bubble IF as before, and redirects kill only the single
instruction in flight behind the branch.

title: 8-entry PC-indexed BTB with isolated mispredict redirect
motivation:
The baseline pays a 2-cycle flush penalty for every taken branch and JAL
because it always fetches PC+4.  Dynamic profiling shows CoreMark executes
~360k taken control-flow instructions out of ~2.9M total, so removing these
bubbles could cut ~720k cycles (~16% IPC gain).  Prior BTB attempts either
produced zero delta (update path disconnected) or broke rvfi_pc_fwd because
the implementation agent altered EX/MEM.pc_next to depend on prediction.
A tiny 8-entry BTB indexed directly by the PC register avoids the slow
imem_data-decode path that killed an earlier static predictor's Fmax, and
keeping the BTB inline in if_stage makes it impossible to forget to
instantiate a separate module.

indexed by pc[4:2] with a 27-bit tag (pc[31:5]).  Combinational read produces
btb_hit and btb_target.  Only BRANCH (opcode 1100011) and JAL (opcode
1101111) are eligible for prediction; JALR is excluded.  The IF next-pc mux

  next_pc = redirect ? redirect_target :
            (btb_hit && is_branch_or_jal) ? btb_target : pc + 4;

overlay, not a replacement.

that JAL (always taken) is never falsely compared against branch_taken:

  jump_mispredict   = ctrl.is_jump   && !predict_taken;

title: Split ALU into fast and M-extension paths with final 2:1 mux
motivation:
A prior hypothesis proved that gating multiply evaluation inside alu.sv
raises Fmax by removing multiplier hardware from the critical path of
non-M ops.  However, the ALU still selects its result through a single
19-entry case statement where the large multiplier/divider outputs are
inputs to the same mux tree as the fast ADD/SUB/LOGIC/SHIFT ops.
Synthesis tools may not fully prune those heavy inputs from the fast-path
mux delay.  By explicitly separating the ALU into a fast path (ADD/SUB/
LOGIC/SHIFT/SLT/LUI) and a slow M-extension path (MUL/DIV/REM) and
selecting between them with a single 2:1 mux, the fast path sees a much
smaller combinational cloud and should achieve a slightly higher Fmax
with zero IPC impact.

Restructure rtl/alu.sv so that:
1. A "fast" always_comb block computes ADD, SUB, AND, OR, XOR, SLT,
   SLTU, SLL, SRL, SRA, and LUI into a local `fast_out` variable.
2. A separate "muldiv" always_comb block (guarded by the existing
   opcode if-gate) computes the 64-bit products and DIV/REM results
   into a local `m_out` variable.
3. A final assignment `out = (op >= ALU_MUL) ? m_out : fast_out;`
   drives the output through a single 2:1 mux instead of a 19:1 case
   statement.  This keeps the ALTOPS branch intact and does not change
   any bit-level results.

title: 2-entry return-address stack for JALR prediction
motivation:
The baseline pays a 2-cycle flush bubble for every taken JALR (function return).
CoreMark is call-heavy, so returns are a significant fraction of dynamic control flow.
Prior BTB attempts either thrashed (8-entry) or were synthesized away (implementation bugs),
and static BTFN predictors destroyed Fmax by adding an imem_data->adder->next_pc critical path.
A tiny 2-entry RAS predicts returns with near-perfect accuracy, avoids an IF-stage adder entirely,
and targets a different instruction class (returns vs branches) than all previous predictor attempts.
Correctly predicted returns eliminate the flush bubble, directly improving IPC.

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Add a 2-entry Return Address Stack (RAS) in core.sv and change EX-stage redirect to
mispredict-only so that correct return predictions do not flush the pipeline.

1. RAS logic in core.sv (top level, no new module):
   - Detect push:  jal with rd==ra  (opcode 1101111, instr[11:7]==1).
   - Detect pop/predict: jalr with rs1==ra (opcode 1100111, instr[19:15]==1).
   - Two 32-bit registers (ras_top, ras_next) plus pointer implied by the swap.
   - On push: ras_next <= ras_top; ras_top <= io_imemAddr + 32'd4.
   - On pop:  ras_top <= ras_next.
   - Updates are gated by !ex_redirect && !flush_if && !stall_if so wrong-path
     instructions during flushes do not corrupt the stack.
   - ras_predict = is_jalr_ra && !ex_redirect && !flush_if && !stall_if.
   - ras_target  = ras_top.

2. Redirect muxing in core.sv:
   - Keep the raw ex_redirect / ex_redirect_target wires from ex_stage.
   - Drive if_stage inputs with combined signals:
       if_redirect       = ex_redirect || ras_predict;
       if_redirect_target = ras_predict ? ras_target : ex_redirect_target;
   - Hazard unit MUST continue to receive the raw ex_redirect (not the combined
     signal) so that correct predictions do not spuriously flush IF/ID.
   - Create an intermediate if_id_t signal from if_stage output, override
     predict_taken = ras_predict and predicted_target = ras_target, then feed
     that to id_stage.  This keeps the RAS out of the timing-critical if_stage
     next-pc path and avoids pc_fwd violations.

3. Pipeline-bundle extension in core_pkg.sv:
   - Add `logic predict_taken;` and `logic [31:0] predicted_target;` to both
     `if_id_t` and `id_ex_t`.

4. ID stage capture in id_stage.sv:
   - In the ID/EX register always_ff, capture:
       reg_q.predict_taken    <= in.predict_taken;
       reg_q.predicted_target <= in.predicted_target;

5. EX-stage mispredict logic in ex_stage.sv:
   - Keep the existing redirect_target and pc_next assignments UNCHANGED.
     (This is the #1 source of prior pc_fwd failures; do not touch them.)
   - Add:
       logic actual_taken = in.ctrl.is_jump || branch_taken;
       logic target_mismatch = in.ctrl.is_jump &&
                               ({jalr_sum[31:1], 1'b0} != in.predicted_target);
       assign redirect = ((actual_taken != in.predict_taken) ||
                          (in.predict_taken && target_mismatch)) && !misalign_fault;
   - For baseline behavior (predict_taken=0, predicted_target=0) this collapses
     to the original redirect expression.
   - On a correctly predicted return, redirect stays low and the pipeline
     continues fetching from the return address without a bubble.
   - On a RAS miss, EX asserts redirect to the true jump target and the
     hazard unit flushes IF/ID as usual.

Cost estimate: ~65 LUTs (32 for the redirect_target mux, ~31 for the 32-bit
equality comparator in EX, plus decode/control).  Previous cores with ~10200
LUTs still evaluated successfully, so the modest area increase is acceptable
for the IPC gain.

title: JAL pre-decode in IF to eliminate unconditional jump bubbles
motivation:
Every JAL currently costs a one-cycle bubble: while JAL travels through ID,
the IF stage blindly fetches PC+4; when JAL reaches EX it redirects, flushing
the wrongly fetched instruction.  JAL is unconditional and its target is
known the moment the instruction is fetched (PC + J-immediate).  Since there
is no mispredict possibility for aligned JAL, the IF stage can safely jump
immediately, removing the bubble entirely without any prediction state or
recovery logic.  This improves IPC for all function calls with no risk of
mispredict penalties.

aligned, override next_pc with the JAL target instead of PC+4.  The PC

because IF already fetched the correct target, there is nothing to flush.
Misaligned JAL falls back to PC+4 in IF, preserving the baseline trap-and-
fallthrough semantics in EX.

title: ICache-integrated JAL and backward-branch predictor
motivation:
The baseline flushes IF/ID on every taken JAL and backward branch, costing 2 cycles each.
Prior predictor attempts failed for three reasons: an 8-entry standalone BTB thrashed on
CoreMark's many active sites (0% IPC gain), a 2-entry RAS decoded io_imemData in core.sv
and fed it back to IF (Fmax crashed to 70 MHz), and a BTFN static predictor broke
rvfi_pc_fwd by altering EX-stage pc_next.  The existing 8-entry I-cache already stores
instructions indexed by PC with tag match.  Extending each entry with a single valid bit
and a 32-bit target reuses the cache's fast read path, stays entirely inside IF, avoids
any combinational decode of external imemData, and costs only ~50 LUT4s.  By predicting
only unconditional JAL and backward branches—whose targets are stable and highly
repetitive in loop-heavy CoreMark—we avoid the aliasing thrashing that neutralized the
prior BTB, while mispredict-only redirect keeps EX's architectural pc_next completely
unchanged, preserving rvfi_pc_fwd.

rvfi_pc_fwd by altering EX-stage pc_next.  The existing 8-entry I-cache already stores
instructions indexed by PC with tag match.  Extending each entry with a single valid bit
and a 32-bit target reuses the cache's fast read path, stays entirely inside IF, avoids
any combinational decode of external imemData, and costs only ~50 LUT4s.  By predicting
only unconditional JAL and backward branches—whose targets are stable and highly
repetitive in loop-heavy CoreMark—we avoid the aliasing thrashing that neutralized the
prior BTB, while mispredict-only redirect keeps EX's architectural pc_next completely
unchanged, preserving rvfi_pc_fwd.
hypothesis.hypothesis:
Integrate a tiny JAL + backward-branch target predictor into the existing 8-entry I-cache.
Each cache entry gains `pred_valid` (1 bit) and `pred_target[31:0]`.  `pred_valid` is
cleared on every cache refill and set only by EX when a JAL or taken backward branch
resolves, storing the resolved target.

In `if_stage`, on a cache hit, the cached instruction is decoded locally (opcode and
sign bit) to decide eligibility:
  `is_jal      = (icache_data[cache_idx][6:0] == 7'b1101111)`
  `is_branch   = (icache_data[cache_idx][6:0] == 7'b1100011)`
  `is_backward = icache_data[cache_idx][31]`
  `predict_taken = icache_hit && pred_valid[cache_idx] && !flush && !redirect &&
                   (is_jal || (is_branch && is_backward))`
`next_pc` becomes `redirect ? redirect_target :
                   predict_taken ? pred_target[cache_idx] : pc + 32'd4`.
Because the predictor reads registered state and reuses the existing cache hit signal,
it does not create the long combinational io_imemData path that destroyed the RAS Fmax.

A single `predict_taken` bit is added to `if_id_t` and `id_ex_t` and captured by
`id_stage`.  In `ex_stage` redirect changes to mispredict-only:
  `actual_taken = in.ctrl.is_jump || branch_taken`
  `redirect     = (!in.predict_taken && actual_taken && !misalign_fault) ||
                  ( in.predict_taken && (!actual_taken || misalign_fault))`
  `redirect_target = misalign_fault          ? (in.pc + 32'd4) :
                     !actual_taken           ? (in.pc + 32'd4) :
                     in.ctrl.is_jump         ? jump_target :
                                               branch_target`
The EX/MEM `pc_next` assignment is left completely untouched, guaranteeing rvfi_pc_fwd.
EX also drives combinational update outputs back to IF:
  `pred_update       = (in.ctrl.is_jump && !in.ctrl.is_jalr) ||
                       (in.ctrl.is_branch && branch_taken && in.imm[31])`
  `pred_update_idx   = in.pc[4:2]`
  `pred_update_tag   = in.pc[31:5]`
  `pred_update_target = redirect_target`
`core.sv` wires the update path and passes `predict_taken` through the struct bundles.
The hazard unit needs no changes—its `redirect` input naturally flushes on mispredict
only, which is exactly the desired behavior.

Cost estimate: ~50 LUT4s and 264 FFs.  Fmax should stay near the current ~165 MHz
because the critical path remains in the ALU fast path and the IF loop sees only a
slightly wider next-pc mux.  IPC should rise 5-10 % by eliminating bubbles on the
most frequent control-flow patterns in CoreMark.

hypothesis.motivation:
The bench ALU currently computes three separate 32×32 products
(signed×signed, unsigned×unsigned, signed×unsigned). Yosys does not
merge them, so the design pays for three multipliers worth of DSP/LUT
area.  With LUT4 already at 9927/10000, placement congestion is severe
and likely limits the achievable Fmax of the fast integer path.
Removing two of the three multipliers frees substantial area without
changing IPC or adding pipeline stages.
hypothesis.hypothesis:
Replace mul_ss, mul_uu and mul_su with a single unsigned×unsigned
product.  MUL/MULHU reuse the low/high halves directly.  MULH and
MULHSU high halves are recovered with a cheap 32-bit correction
subtractor: MULHSU subtracts b when a is negative; MULH additionally
subtracts a when b is negative.  The ALTOPS formal branch and the
divider path are untouched, and the fast-path 2:1 mux is preserved.

title: ICache-integrated JAL/backward-branch predictor with tag-matched retention
motivation:
The baseline with an 8-entry I-cache and ALU fast path achieves 396.13 fitness,
but still flushes IF/ID on every taken JAL and backward branch, costing two cycles.
A prior attempt to integrate target prediction into the I-cache (hyp-20260514-003-r9s2)
produced exactly 0 % delta because the predictor valid bit was cleared on every
cache refill, so predictions were never visible when the branch PC was fetched again.
By storing a partial tag with each prediction entry and qualifying the hit with
tag equality, the predictor state survives across repeated fetches and evictions
without needing explicit invalidation logic.  This reuses the existing fast I-cache
read path (registered state, no external imem_data combinational delay) and adds
only ~250 FFs and a small next-pc mux, well within the remaining LUT4 headroom.
